story:
A blonde girl wakes on a cottage floor, gobsmacked, staring at a red‑headed boy tied to a chair in a cozy living room. Two other girls rouse beside her—a sharp‑eyed brunette and a ginger in a short white dress—and a chinchilla in a top hat and bow tie is eating peanut butter straight from a jar with a spoon. The boy, about fourteen, panics at his ropes; the girls, about twelve, insist they won’t hurt him. The chinchilla, who calls himself Peanut and speaks in a stretched, third‑person drawl—“Peaaanut is offended. Offended, indeed.”—declares the boy a coward. The boy gapes at a talking animal. The blonde offers a cuppa; the brunette rolls her eyes. Reality flexes.

The blonde explains: they’re characters in a story. When the author writes, they black out and perform whatever’s written, then remember nothing. When the writing stops, they’re themselves again. The boy was created in the last blackout—so he’s at most a day old—and Peanut talks because the author made him talk. The boy asks if the author also made Peanut rude; the chinchilla splutters, peanut butter on his chin, and the room explodes with laughter. The boy and the blonde catch a spark and blush. The girls name him Axel. Introductions: Armelle (brunette), Elody (blonde), Ruby (ginger). They untie him.

They’re planning to escape the story, into the real world. They’ve run experiments and kept a thick journal. The author, they suspect, wrote the blackouts and has probably meddled again: the journal is missing from the desk upstairs, by a big window that looks onto a modest house and, beyond it, a hulking mansion beside their tiny cottage. They split up to search. Peanut finds only a single sheet of paper. Morale dips.

The invisible wall they’d tested sits at the forest’s edge; they think it’s a dome. At first they thought nothing lay beyond it. Later they guessed the dome might brush the real world. Elody rethinks: the real world wouldn’t be crammed behind every story’s fence. Maybe you have to fly or teleport there. Flight won’t help under a dome. Teleportation? They’ve no clue where to start. Ruby reaches for the sheet in Peanut’s tiny paws. Just as they lean over it—the world goes black.

They come to on the grass. Axel is missing. They scour the cottage and the modest house. In the mansion’s marble foyer, Elody steadies herself and sees Axel’s lifeless body. Grief crashes over them. They blame the author; Elody is terrified her written self might have done it. They sit for hours, eyes raw and empty. Even Peanut just eats.

At last Peanut murmurs that Axel would want them to keep trying. He leads them back to the cottage. Upstairs, he slides open a desk drawer and produces the thin sheet.

Ruby hatches a plan: if things happen when the author writes them, maybe writing on the author’s own paper can bend their world. Her sentence won’t force anyone’s actions, so no blackout. Elody, eyes lighting again, begs her to bring Axel back. Ruby grabs a pen and writes, “Axel comes back to life just as a magical portal to the real world opens up on this very page.”

At first, nothing. Then a black dot blooms into a swirling page‑sized hole, full of stars and drifting rock. The girls cheer. Peanut dances, doffing his top hat and twirling it. Elody sprints for the mansion, flings open the door, and beams at the empty floor where a body should be. In another room, a familiar red‑headed boy looks sheepish.

“Hey,” Axel says—and breath returns to their world.
